title: DAVID Deep Dive — Proto-Indo-European: The Mother Tongue

shots.speech_text: Six thousand years ago, one language split into hundreds. We never wrote it down. But its echo is still in your mouth every time you say sheep, mother, or three.
shots.video_prompt: Archive host speaks directly to camera in English, lip-synced, slow documentary gravitas, subtle push-in, warm brass lamp, open codex on worktable. Delivers hook about language family and everyday English words.

shots.speech_text: What did they actually say? … And how do we prove it?
shots.video_prompt: Host medium-close, signature beat, thoughtful pause between clauses, still grounded at Archive desk, trustworthy delivery.

shots.speech_text: Proto-Indo-European is not a lost manuscript. It's a hypothesis built from daughter languages — Sanskrit, Greek, Latin, Old English, and dozens more.
shots.video_prompt: Host explains PIE concept in English, gestures toward manuscripts, educational tone, steady medium shot.

shots.speech_text: There is no recording. No native speaker. Every PIE word you hear carries an asterisk — the scholarly mark for reconstruction.
shots.video_prompt: Host speaks with calm honesty about absence of attestation, on-screen NOT ATTESTED feel, documentary authority.

shots.speech_text: In 1868 August Schleicher wrote a little fable in reconstructed PIE — a sheep and some horses on a journey. It's our standard teaching excerpt.
shots.video_prompt: Host introduces Schleicher fable, points to codex, measured pace, Archive setting consistent.
Host speaks reconstructed PIE line slowly, lip-synced, scholarly tone, never claiming recording. IPA target [ˈh₂é.wis ˈh₁é.kʷoːs ˈh₁é.kʷon.tes]. Bold RECONSTRUCTED tags implied.

shots.on_screen: *h₂éwis → Latin ovis → Sanskrit ávi- → English ewe
shots.speech_text: The word for sheep: Latin ovis, Sanskrit ávi-, English ewe — all point backward to a reconstructed root *h₂éwis.
shots.video_prompt: Host teaches cognate chain for sheep, gestures to table, didactic English delivery.

shots.on_screen: *h₁éḱwos → Latin equus → Greek ἵππος → English equestrian
shots.speech_text: Horses: Latin equus, Greek hippos, English equestrian — daughters of *h₁éḱwos, with a debated laryngeal in the middle.
shots.video_prompt: Host explains horse cognate family, calm authority, one idea per shot.

shots.speech_text: Those h-with-subscript symbols? Laryngeals — consonants we infer never left a direct letter, only shadows in daughter languages.
shots.video_prompt: Host introduces laryngeal theory symbols, honest uncertainty, educational documentary tone.

shots.on_screen: Comparative method · work backward from daughters
shots.speech_text: We line up related words across languages, find the sound laws that changed them, and reconstruct the parent form that best explains the pattern.
shots.video_prompt: Host explains comparative method, gestures toward multilingual manuscripts, measured gravitas.

shots.speech_text: Sanskrit often preserves archaic features — which is why nineteenth-century scholars turned to India to crack the Indo-European puzzle.
shots.video_prompt: Host discusses Sanskrit role in IE studies, warm lamp light, scholarly tone.

shots.on_screen: IE branches · Germanic · Italic · Indo-Iranian · Slavic · Celtic…
shots.speech_text: From one ancestor: Germanic, Italic, Celtic, Slavic, Indo-Iranian, and more — a family tree you can still hear in grammar and vocabulary.
shots.video_prompt: Host surveys IE branches, wide medium shot of Archive, map-like energy without literal graphics.

shots.speech_text: This is rigorous inference, not time travel. DAVID always labels reconstructed forms — because intellectual honesty is the whole point.
shots.video_prompt: Host states honesty principle, direct eye contact, documentary trust signal.

shots.speech_text: Phonologists still argue details — which laryngeal where, what accent, which dialect stage. Reconstruction is a living science.
shots.video_prompt: Host acknowledges scholarly debate, measured pace, no false certainty.

shots.speech_text: Bring the dead tongues back — one attestation at a time. DAVID.
shots.video_prompt: Host warm close, tagline delivery, slight smile of quiet wonder, medium close-up.

provenance_card.title: DAVID · PROVENANCE · PIE Deep Dive
provenance_card.subtitle: Proto-Indo-European — Schleicher fable opener
lines: TEXT [reconstructed]: August Schleicher, Compendium der vergleichenden Grammatik (1861/62); modern recension Schleicher's Fable. Reconstructed — not attested.
PRONUNCIATION [reconstructed]: reconstructed late PIE phonology (laryngeal theory); moderate confidence — scholarly consensus with debated detail.
TRANSLATION: The sheep and the horses (were) going…
SOURCES: Wikipedia / Wiktionary (CC BY-SA), Wikidata (CC0), retrieved 2026-06-18.
provenance_card.footer: reconstructed text · reconstructed pronunciation · comparative method

provenance.text: August Schleicher, Compendium der vergleichenden Grammatik (1861/62); modern recension Schleicher's Fable. Reconstructed — not attested.
provenance.pronunciation: reconstructed late PIE phonology (laryngeal theory); moderate — scholarly consensus with debated laryngeal detail.
